text.title: Chaos Engineering Practice for Infra Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of chaos-engineering-practice-for-infra enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Every experiment ships with a written hypothesis stating the expected steady-state behaviour and the predicted observable on failure.
rule.rationale: Without a hypothesis, an experiment is just sabotage; with one, the outcome teaches whether the mental model is correct.

rule.statement: Every experiment has bounded blast radius — explicit list of affected services / users / regions — and exec sign-off for production-wide scope.
rule.rationale: Unbounded chaos is incident; bounded chaos is learning.

rule.statement: Every experiment has automated abort criteria tied to SLO thresholds; tripping the abort halts the experiment without human intervention.
rule.rationale: Manual aborts are slow; automated aborts make chaos production-safe.

rule.statement: Every learning is converted to a regression test (synthetic check, alert, or automated chaos run) so the system can never regress silently.
rule.rationale: Without conversion, learnings rot in postmortems and recur 18 months later.

rule.statement: Experiments run on a cadence (e.g. weekly small + quarterly GameDay), not just when incidents prompt them.

rationale.source: Principles of Chaos Engineering 2020 — Run experiments in production

rule.rationale: Cadence builds muscle memory across the team; one-off experiments do not.

rule.statement: If preconditions in `Applies If` are not all true, skip this methodology and route to the adjacent one referenced in Related.
rule.rationale: A skip rule keeps the decision tree closed: every branch ends at a real rule id.
